chunk_summary: This section addresses nipple and infant oral candidiasis dyad treatment, emphasizing accurate diagnosis, combined mother-infant therapy, antifungal protocols, and preventive measures to avoid reinfection during breastfeeding under 2 years.
learning_objectives: Understand the differential diagnosis for persistent nipple pain during breastfeeding.
Learn appropriate antifungal treatments for both mother and infant in candidiasis cases.
Identify preventive measures to reduce recurrence of nipple and oral candidiasis.
Recognize urgent signs requiring escalation, such as vesicular lesions or infant dehydration.
key_facts: Candida is often overdiagnosed; reassess latch, trauma, vasospasm, dermatitis, and bacterial infection first.
Infant treatment: Nystatin suspension or miconazole oral gel per local protocol; continue therapy beyond plaque disappearance.
Maternal treatment: Topical azoles (clotrimazole or miconazole) after feeds; oral fluconazole (200 mg once, then 100 mg daily for 7–10 days) for resistant cases.
Fluconazole transfer into milk is below therapeutic infant dosing and compatible with breastfeeding.
Sterilize or replace items contacting milk (e.g., pump parts, pacifiers) to prevent reinfection.
reference_quote: Fluconazole transfer into milk is generally below therapeutic infant dosing and is considered compatible with breastfeeding; however, maternal dosing will not treat infant thrush, so infant therapy must be separate. (Source: LactMed Fluconazole Database)
document_key_topics: Differential diagnosis for nipple pain during breastfeeding
